document.source_title: What Does Putin Want From Xi?
document.summary: Russian President Vladimir Putin visited China for a two-day summit to reaffirm the Sino-Russian partnership amid geopolitical tensions. This visit marks the 25th time Putin has traveled to China as president, coinciding with the 25th anniversary of the Sino-Russian Treaty of Friendship. Despite the strong rhetoric of partnership, Russia's economic troubles have made the relationship increasingly lopsided, with Moscow becoming more dependent on Beijing for energy exports.
key_takeaways: Putin's visit to China aims to strengthen bilateral ties amid geopolitical challenges.
China has purchased over $367 billion in Russian fossil fuels since the Ukraine war began.
The Power of Siberia 2 pipeline is set to enhance Russian gas exports to China.
Putin's aide reported a 35% increase in Russian oil exports to China in early 2026.
China maintains a neutral stance in the Russia-Ukraine conflict, despite allegations of military training.
Xi Jinping's recent comments suggest a potential shift in China's support for Russia.
document.why_it_matters: The evolving dynamics of the China-Russia relationship are crucial for understanding global energy markets and geopolitical alliances, especially in the context of the ongoing Ukraine conflict and Western sanctions against Russia.

claims.claim_text: Russia's economic troubles have made its partnership with China increasingly lopsided.
claims.evidence_text: Russia’s growing economic troubles—from its war in Ukraine to the fallout of conflict in the Middle East—have transformed what the Kremlin considers to be an equal partnership into a more lopsided affair.

claims.claim_text: China has purchased over $367 billion worth of Russian fossil fuels since February 2022.
claims.evidence_text: Despite Western sanctions, China has purchased more than $367 billion worth of Russian fossil fuels since the Russia-Ukraine war began in February 2022.

claims.claim_text: Putin's aide reported a 35% increase in Russian oil exports to China in early 2026.
claims.evidence_text: During the first quarter of 2026, Russian oil exports to China grew by 35 percent, according to Putin’s presidential aide Yuri Ushakov.

claims.claim_text: China has not provided lethal weapons to either side in the Russia-Ukraine war.
claims.evidence_text: Beijing maintains that it has never provided lethal weapons to either side of the Russia-Ukraine war.

entities.name: Vladimir Putin
entities.description: President of Russia, visiting China for a summit.
entities.evidence_text: Russian President Vladimir Putin—long dubbed an “old friend” of Xi’s—arrived in China late Tuesday for a two-day summit.

entities.name: Xi Jinping
entities.description: President of China, hosting the summit with Putin.
entities.evidence_text: Chinese President Xi Jinping hosted U.S. President Donald Trump, Beijing welcomed another world leader.

entities.name: Sino-Russian Treaty of Friendship
entities.description: A treaty marking 25 years of partnership between China and Russia.
entities.evidence_text: Putin’s visit coincides with the 25th anniversary of the Sino-Russian Treaty of Friendship.

events.event: Putin's visit to China for a summit
events.evidence_text: Russian President Vladimir Putin—long dubbed an “old friend” of Xi’s—arrived in China late Tuesday for a two-day summit.

relationships.subject: Russia
relationships.evidence_text: Moscow becomes increasingly dependent on Beijing.
